name: Herbal Flea Spray for Dogs
description: Numerous clinical trials in dogs have shown the potential health benefits of turmeric. It has shown to be a potent anti-inflammatory and is also a good pain reliever. It has even been shown to have a positive effect on cancer cells. If you pet has any inflammatory disease, arthritis or cancer, you may want to give turmeric a try. This whole food recipe, created by Dr. Doug English, an Australian Veterinarian, is a wonderful way to safely incorporate turmeric into your furry friend's holistic healthcare plan.
ingredients: 1 cup water, plus 1 cup water in reserve, if needed
1/3 cup raw/cold pressed/unrefined Coconut/Flaxseed/Linseed/Extra-Virgin Olive Oil
instructions: Place turmeric and water in pan, stirring over gentle heat until you have a thick paste. This should take about 7-10 minutes and you may need to add additional water along the way.
Add the freshly ground pepper and oil AT THE END of cooking.
Stir well (a whisk is ideal) to incorporate the oil and allow to cool.
A good starting dose is 1/4 teaspoon two to three times daily. Just add to your dog's food.
benefits: May have a positive effect for cancer.
caution: Caution for use in dogs with gallbladder stones or bile duct obstruction. Also use with cation in diabetic patients. Stomach ulcers and bleeding disorders are two other situations where turmeric is not recommended. It can occasionally cause stomach upset and might slow blood clotting. These effects are all very mild but if your pet has any of these conditions, then turmeric would not be the best choice for them.
storageNotes: The Golden Paste will keep for 2 weeks, refrigerated. Freeze a portion if you think you have too much to use within two weeks.

name: 🌿🐾 Strong Herbal Flea‑Repelling Dog Shampoo
description: Best for: dogs with recurring flea issues, outdoor dogs, or dogs in warm/humid climates like Florida.
ingredients: 1 tablespoon dried rosemary (strong natural flea repellent)
½ cup liquid Castile soap (unscented or mild)
1 tablespoon aloe vera gel (soothes irritated skin)
Optional: 2–3 drops cedarwood or lemongrass essential oil*
*Use only if your dog is over 12 weeks old and not sensitive to scents.
instructions: Make the herbal concentrate:
1. Bring the 2 cups of water to a boil.
2. Add rosemary, mint, and lavender.
3. Turn off heat, cover, and steep 20–30 minutes for maximum strength.
4. Strain out herbs and let the liquid cool completely.
Mix the shampoo:
1. In a bottle or jar, combine the cooled herbal tea with the Castile soap.
2. Add aloe vera gel and optional essential oil.
3. Shake gently to blend.
To Use it:
1. Wet your dog thoroughly.
2. Massage shampoo into the coat, especially neck, tail base, and belly.
3. Let it sit 3–5 minutes so the herbs and soap can work.
4. Rinse well.
benefits: -Rosemary + mint create a scent profile fleas avoid.
-Lavender calms skin and adds mild repellent action.
-Cedarwood or lemongrass (optional) significantly increase repellent strength.
-Herbal infusion penetrates the coat better than essential oils alone.
caution: Avoid essential oils for puppies under 12 weeks, pregnant dogs, or dogs with respiratory issues.  Never use this on cats — several of these herbs/oils are unsafe for them.  Store in the fridge for up to 2 weeks.

name: Lemongrass & Ginger Digestive Tea
description: A soothing and aromatic tea that calms the stomach, reduces bloating, and aids digestion. One of the most popular herbal teas you can make straight from your Zone 10b garden.
ingredients: 1-inch piece fresh ginger root, sliced thin
instructions: Cut the bottom 6 inches of lemongrass stalks and peel off the outer layers. Bruise the stalks by pressing firmly with the back of a knife — this releases the oils.
Slice ginger root thin (no need to peel if from your own garden).
Bring 2 cups of water to a boil in a small saucepan.
Add lemongrass and ginger. Reduce heat and simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Strain into cups. Add honey and lime if desired.
Drink warm for digestive issues or chilled over ice for a refreshing drink.
caution: Generally very safe. Avoid large amounts during pregnancy. Ginger may interact with blood thinners — consult your doctor if on medication.
storageNotes: Make a larger batch and refrigerate for up to 5 days. Reheat or serve cold.

name: Holy Basil (Tulsi) Adaptogenic Tea
description: Tulsi (Holy Basil) is an Ayurvedic 'adaptogen' — it helps the body handle stress and boosts immunity. This calming tea is excellent for stress relief, anxiety, and immune support.
instructions: Rinse fresh tulsi leaves thoroughly.
Place leaves in a mug or teapot.
Pour boiling water over the leaves.
Cover and steep for 10-15 minutes (longer = stronger).
Strain. Add honey, black pepper, and lemon.
Drink 1-2 cups daily for best adaptogenic benefits.
caution: May lower blood sugar — monitor if diabetic. Avoid medicinal doses during pregnancy. May interact with blood thinners.
storageNotes: Best consumed fresh. Refrigerate leftover tea for up to 2 days.

name: Turmeric Golden Milk
description: A warming anti-inflammatory drink using fresh-harvested turmeric from your garden. The black pepper is essential — it increases curcumin (turmeric's active compound) absorption by 2,000%.
ingredients: 1 tsp fresh turmeric root, grated (or 1/2 tsp dried powder)
1 cup coconut milk or whole milk
Pinch of black pepper (ESSENTIAL — enhances absorption)
instructions: Combine all ingredients in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Whisk constantly while heating — do not boil.
Heat until steaming and well combined, about 3-5 minutes.
Pour through a fine strainer into a mug.
Adjust sweetness to taste.
Serve immediately. Best enjoyed before bed.
benefits: Aids sleep when taken at night
caution: Turmeric stains everything it touches — use care with clothing and surfaces. Avoid high doses during pregnancy or if on blood thinners. The black pepper is not optional if you want the benefits.
storageNotes: Make a turmeric paste ahead of time: 1/4 cup turmeric + enough water to form a paste. Store in fridge 2 weeks, add to milk as needed.

name: Rosemary Memory & Focus Tea
description: Rosemary has been used for centuries to enhance memory and mental clarity. This refreshing tea combines rosemary's cognitive benefits with a pleasant herbal flavor. Great as a morning drink.
instructions: Rinse rosemary sprigs. Bruise lightly between your fingers to release oils.
Place in a mug or teapot and pour boiling water over.
Steep covered for 8-10 minutes (no longer — can become bitter).
Strain and add honey and lemon.
Drink in the morning for cognitive benefits.
caution: Do not use medicinal amounts during pregnancy. High doses may cause seizures in susceptible individuals. Culinary amounts in tea are generally safe.
storageNotes: Refrigerate for up to 3 days. Also excellent as a cold-brewed tea — steep in cold water in the fridge overnight.

name: Roselle Hibiscus Flower Tea (Agua de Jamaica)
description: Tangy, ruby-red herbal tea made from the calyces of Roselle hibiscus growing in your garden. Loaded with antioxidants and vitamin C. Shown in studies to help lower blood pressure. Delicious hot or cold.
ingredients: 1 cup fresh roselle calyces (the red parts around the seed pod), or 1/2 cup dried
2-3 tbsp sugar, honey, or agave (to taste)
instructions: Harvest roselle calyces when the seed pods form — the red fleshy parts peel away from the seed pod.
Rinse the calyces well.
Bring water to a boil. Add calyces (and ginger if using).
Simmer 15-20 minutes until water is deeply ruby-red.
Strain and stir in sweetener and lime juice while hot.
Serve hot or refrigerate and serve over ice.
Garnish with mint.
benefits: High in vitamin C and antioxidants
caution: May lower blood pressure — consult doctor if on blood pressure medication. Avoid large amounts during pregnancy. The sour taste comes from natural acids — dilute for children.
storageNotes: Refrigerates well for up to 1 week. Concentrate can be made and diluted with water as needed. Excellent frozen into popsicles.

name: Moringa Leaf Superfood Tea
description: Moringa is called the 'Miracle Tree' for good reason — its leaves are packed with iron, calcium, potassium, and vitamins A, C, and E. This mild, slightly grassy tea is an easy way to add remarkable nutrition to your day.
ingredients: 1 tbsp fresh moringa leaves, loosely packed (or 1 tsp dried powder)
2 cups hot water (not boiling — 170-180°F preserves nutrients)
instructions: Harvest fresh moringa leaves in the morning when nutrient content is highest.
Rinse thoroughly. Use fresh or dry in the sun for 2 days for a shelf-stable powder.
Heat water to just below boiling (high boil destroys some nutrients).
Add moringa leaves or powder to mug. Pour hot water over.
Steep 5-7 minutes. Strain if using whole leaves.
Add honey and citrus. Drink 1-2 cups daily.
benefits: Exceptionally high in iron and calcium
caution: Avoid very high doses during pregnancy (the roots and bark can cause contractions — leaves are safer in culinary amounts). May interact with thyroid medications.
storageNotes: Make a large batch of dried moringa powder and store in an airtight jar away from light for up to 6 months.

name: Aloe Vera Cooling Skin Tincture
description: A shelf-stable concentrated aloe preparation for sunburns, minor burns, skin irritation, and bug bites. The alcohol base preserves the gel and extracts additional beneficial compounds.
ingredients: 1 cup 80-proof vodka or grain alcohol (do NOT use rubbing alcohol)
1 small dark glass bottle with dropper
instructions: Cut mature outer leaves from the plant. Stand them upright in a jar for 15 minutes to drain the yellow aloin latex (discard the yellow liquid — it's a laxative).
Slice off the green skin on both sides. Scoop out only the clear inner gel.
Blend the clear gel until smooth.
Mix gel with vodka in a 1:1 ratio by volume.
Pour into a clean jar, seal, and store in a dark cool place.
Shake daily for 2 weeks.
Strain through cheesecloth into a dark dropper bottle.
Label with date. Apply topically to burns, sunburns, or irritated skin.
caution: For external use only. Never consume alcohol-based tinctures internally without medical guidance. Avoid yellow aloin layer — it is a strong laxative and skin irritant. Always do a patch test first.
storageNotes: Store in a dark glass bottle away from heat and light. Shelf-stable for 1-2 years. Label clearly.

name: Neem Leaf Garden Pest Spray
description: A powerful natural insecticide and fungicide made from neem leaves from your garden. Effective against aphids, whiteflies, spider mites, scale, and fungal diseases. Safe for beneficial insects when used correctly.
ingredients: 2 cups fresh neem leaves, loosely packed
OR: 2 tbsp neem oil + 1 tsp dish soap + 1 quart water (faster method)
instructions: LEAF METHOD: Bring 2 cups water to a boil. Remove from heat.
Add neem leaves and steep covered for 30 minutes.
Strain. Let cool completely.
Add dish soap. Pour into spray bottle.
Spray on affected plants, especially leaf undersides, in the early morning or evening — never in direct sun.
OIL METHOD: Combine neem oil, dish soap, and water. Shake well before each use.
Reapply every 7 days for 3-4 weeks to break pest cycles.
benefits: Safe around people and pets when dry
Does not harm beneficial insects when applied correctly
caution: Harmful to fish and aquatic insects — do not apply near water bodies. Apply only in the morning or evening to avoid harming pollinators. Never apply in direct sun — can burn leaves.
storageNotes: Use leaf infusion within 3-4 days. Neem oil spray can be stored 1 week in the refrigerator. Shake well before each use.

name: Turmeric & Ginger Anti-Inflammatory Tincture
description: A potent concentrated herbal extract for joint pain, inflammation, and digestive issues. Using both turmeric and ginger from your garden creates a powerful synergistic anti-inflammatory effect.
ingredients: 1/2 cup fresh turmeric root, chopped small
1/4 cup fresh ginger root, chopped small
1.5 cups 80-proof vodka or apple cider vinegar (ACV for alcohol-free)
1 dark glass jar with tight lid
instructions: Wash and chop turmeric and ginger — no need to peel if from your garden.
Place roots and peppercorns in a clean glass jar.
Pour vodka or ACV over the roots until completely submerged. Add more if needed.
Seal tightly. Label with date and contents.
Store in a dark, cool place. Shake once daily.
After 4-6 weeks, strain through cheesecloth, pressing the roots to extract all liquid.
Pour into a dark dropper bottle.
DOSE: 1-2 dropperfuls (about 1/4 tsp) in water or tea, 2-3 times daily.
benefits: Powerful anti-inflammatory for joints and muscles
Black pepper increases curcumin absorption by 2,000%
caution: Avoid during pregnancy. May thin blood — consult doctor before use with blood thinners or blood sugar medications. Turmeric stains — protect surfaces and clothing.
storageNotes: Alcohol-based tincture is shelf-stable for 3-5 years. ACV version keeps for 1 year. Store in dark glass away from light and heat.

name: Aloe & Garden Herb Healing Salve
description: A multipurpose skin salve for dry skin, minor cuts, sunburn, insect bites, and chapped lips using aloe from your garden. This beeswax-based salve locks in the healing properties and creates a protective barrier.
yield: 4 oz (fills a small tin or jar)
ingredients: 1/4 cup aloe vera gel (fresh inner gel)
1 tbsp dried rosemary (infused in oil — optional)
5 drops tea tree oil (optional — for antimicrobial action)
Small metal tins or glass jars for storage
instructions: INFUSE THE OIL (optional): Gently warm coconut oil with rosemary on the lowest heat setting for 30-60 minutes. Strain and discard the herbs.
Melt beeswax in a double boiler (or bowl over hot water).
Once melted, add coconut oil and stir until fully combined.
Remove from heat. Let cool slightly (2-3 minutes) but not solidify.
Quickly stir in fresh aloe vera gel. It will sputter slightly — this is normal.
Add essential oils and stir well.
Pour immediately into tins or jars before it solidifies.
Let cool completely undisturbed (1-2 hours) before using or covering.
Apply a thin layer to affected skin as needed.
benefits: Antimicrobial properties from rosemary and tea tree
caution: Do not apply to deep wounds or infected skin. Discontinue if irritation occurs. Avoid near eyes. Always do a patch test on a small skin area first.
storageNotes: Store in a cool dark place. Shelf life 3-6 months at room temperature, up to 1 year refrigerated. The aloe may cause slight color variation — normal.

name: Neem & Rosemary Insect-Repellent Salve
description: A natural insect-repellent balm using neem and rosemary from your garden. Apply to exposed skin to deter mosquitoes, gnats, and other insects. Safe and pleasant-smelling alternative to DEET-based sprays.
ingredients: 1 tbsp dried rosemary leaves, finely ground
instructions: Melt beeswax in a double boiler.
Add coconut oil and neem oil. Stir until fully combined.
Stir in ground rosemary.
Remove from heat. Cool for 2 minutes.
Add all essential oils and stir quickly.
Pour into small tins immediately.
Cool completely before use.
Apply a thin layer to exposed skin before going outside.
benefits: Neem oil compounds repel over 200 insect species
Rosemary and eucalyptus enhance repellent effect
caution: Neem has a strong odor that some find unpleasant — the essential oils help mask it. Do not use on broken skin. Avoid contact with eyes. Test on small skin area first — neem can cause irritation in sensitive individuals. Not recommended for children under 2.
storageNotes: Store in a cool, dark place. Good for 6 months at room temperature.

name: Ginger Honey Fire Cider (Immune Syrup)
description: A traditional folk remedy and powerful immune-boosting tonic using fresh ginger and other garden herbs. Take a tablespoon daily during cold/flu season or at the first sign of illness. Tastes like a spicy sweet punch.
ingredients: 1-2 hot peppers (optional — cayenne or bird's eye)
Apple cider vinegar (raw, with mother) — enough to cover
instructions: Prepare all ingredients and place everything except honey and vinegar into a quart mason jar.
Pour apple cider vinegar over everything until all ingredients are completely submerged.
Seal with a plastic lid (vinegar corrodes metal lids) and label with date.
Store in a dark cool place for 3-4 weeks. Shake daily.
After infusing, strain through cheesecloth pressing firmly to extract all liquid.
Stir in raw honey to taste — traditionally equal parts strained liquid to honey.
Bottle in dark glass. Take 1-2 tablespoons daily as a tonic.
Can also be added to salad dressings, smoothies, or taken as a shot.
benefits: Rich in antioxidants and probiotics from raw ACV
caution: Very spicy — start with 1 teaspoon and work up. Avoid on empty stomach. Not for young children or those with acid reflux. Consult doctor if on blood thinners.
storageNotes: Refrigerate after making. Keeps 2-3 months refrigerated. The vinegar acts as a natural preservative.

name: Lemongrass Citrus Relaxing Body Oil
description: A light aromatic body oil using lemongrass from your garden, perfect for after-bath moisturizing, massage, and stress relief. The fresh citrusy scent of homegrown lemongrass is far superior to store-bought versions.
ingredients: 1/2 cup sweet almond oil or fractionated coconut oil
10 drops lemon essential oil (optional — enhances scent)
instructions: COLD INFUSION METHOD: Place sliced lemongrass in a clean glass jar. Cover completely with carrier oil. Seal and store in a sunny windowsill for 1-2 weeks. Strain out plant material.
QUICK HEAT METHOD: Combine lemongrass and oil in a small saucepan. Heat on lowest setting for 2-3 hours (oil should be warm, not hot). Strain through cheesecloth.
Add essential oils to the strained oil if desired.
Pour into a dark glass bottle.
Apply to skin after shower while skin is still slightly damp for best absorption.
Also excellent as a massage oil for sore muscles.
benefits: Muscle tension relief when used as massage oil
Antifungal and antibacterial properties of lemongrass
caution: Do not apply to broken or irritated skin. Avoid contact with eyes. Do a patch test before full application — lemongrass can irritate sensitive skin. Not for internal use.
storageNotes: Store in a dark glass bottle away from heat. Good for 6-12 months. Refrigerate for longer shelf life.

name: Moringa Garden Green Smoothie
description: A nutrient-packed smoothie using fresh moringa leaves from your garden. Moringa contains all essential amino acids, making this one of the most nutritionally complete plant-based smoothies possible.
ingredients: 1 cup fresh moringa leaves, loosely packed (or 1 tsp moringa powder)
instructions: Harvest fresh moringa leaves in the morning. Rinse well.
Remove leaves from stems (stems are fibrous and affect texture).
Add all ingredients to a blender.
Blend on high for 60 seconds until completely smooth.
Taste and adjust sweetness.
Pour over ice and drink immediately for maximum nutrition.
The banana and pineapple effectively mask moringa's mild bitter taste.
benefits: Complete plant protein with all essential amino acids
Exceptionally high in iron, calcium, and vitamin C
Easy daily nutrition from your garden
caution: Avoid very large amounts during pregnancy (moringa bark and root can stimulate contractions — leaves in culinary amounts are generally safe). May interact with thyroid medication in large doses.
storageNotes: Drink immediately for best nutrition. Smoothie keeps in refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

name: Passion Fruit & Hibiscus Drinking Shrub
description: A 'shrub' is a drinking vinegar — a preserved fruit syrup with vinegar that creates a refreshing, slightly tangy drink. This stunning deep-pink shrub uses passion fruit and roselle hibiscus from your garden.
ingredients: 1 cup ripe passion fruit pulp (about 8-10 fruits)
1/2 cup fresh roselle calyces (or 1/4 cup dried)
1 cup raw cane sugar or honey
instructions: Scoop passion fruit pulp from shells. Combine with roselle calyces, sugar, and water in a saucepan.
Bring to a gentle simmer, stirring until sugar dissolves.
Simmer 5 minutes to extract flavor from roselle.
Remove from heat and add vinegar.
Cool completely, then strain into a glass jar.
Refrigerate for 3-5 days to mellow and develop flavor.
TO SERVE: Mix 1-2 oz shrub with 6-8 oz sparkling water over ice.
Also excellent as a cocktail mixer or salad dressing base.
benefits: Rich in vitamin C from roselle
caution: High sugar content — use honey for a lower glycemic option. Vinegar-based — avoid if you have acid reflux. The tartness is intentional; dilute to taste.
storageNotes: Refrigerated shrub keeps 6 months or longer. The vinegar is a natural preservative. Can also be stored at room temperature for 2-3 months.

name: Acerola Cherry Vitamin C Elixir
description: Fresh acerola cherries from your garden contain up to 100x more vitamin C than oranges. This simple elixir is one of the most potent natural vitamin C supplements you can make. Take it at the first sign of a cold.
instructions: Harvest acerola when bright red and slightly soft — this is peak vitamin C and flavor.
Remove stems and pits.
Blend cherries with water until smooth.
Strain through a fine-mesh strainer, pressing pulp to extract all juice.
Stir in honey, lime juice, salt, and ginger if using.
Drink immediately — vitamin C degrades quickly when exposed to light and air.
For maximum potency, prepare and drink fresh daily.
benefits: Extraordinarily high vitamin C content (1,000-4,500 mg per 100g)
caution: Acerola is very high in vitamin C — excessive amounts (more than 1-2 cups) may cause digestive upset or loose stools. Start with small amounts. Cherries are very perishable — use within 24 hours of harvest.
storageNotes: Fresh elixir is best consumed immediately. Can freeze acerola cherries whole for year-round use — vitamin C is largely preserved when frozen.

name: Tamarind Agua Fresca
description: A classic refreshing drink from the Caribbean and Latin America using tamarind pods from your garden. Sweet, tart, and deeply satisfying — especially in Florida's heat. Far superior to commercial versions made from concentrates.
ingredients: 6-8 ripe tamarind pods (or 4 oz tamarind paste/block)
Ice and lime wedges to serve
instructions: Shell the tamarind pods and remove fibrous veins.
Soak tamarind pulp in 2 cups warm water for 20 minutes until soft.
Work the pulp with your hands to dissolve it, removing seeds.
Strain through a fine-mesh strainer, pressing firmly to extract all pulp.
Add remaining 4 cups cold water, sugar, and salt.
Stir until sugar dissolves. Taste and adjust sweetness.
Serve over ice with lime wedges.
Stir before serving as tamarind settles.
benefits: Rich in B vitamins and minerals
Natural cooling drink for hot weather
caution: High in natural sugars — moderate consumption for diabetics. The very tart flavor is normal — adjust sugar to taste. May act as a mild laxative in large amounts.
storageNotes: Refrigerates well for up to 5 days. Stir or shake before serving. Can be made as a concentrate and diluted with water as needed.

name: Nasturtium Peppery Garden Pesto
description: A vibrant, peppery pesto using nasturtium leaves and flowers from your garden. Nasturtium has a similar heat to watercress with notes of mustard. Stunning orange and yellow color. Use on pasta, sandwiches, or as a dip.
ingredients: 2 cups fresh nasturtium leaves and flowers, loosely packed
1/4 cup Parmesan cheese (or nutritional yeast for vegan)
instructions: Rinse nasturtium leaves and flowers. Pat dry.
Toast nuts in a dry pan over medium heat until golden, 3-4 minutes.
Add nasturtium, basil, garlic, and nuts to a food processor.
Pulse several times to break down.
With motor running, slowly drizzle in olive oil until smooth.
Add Parmesan, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Pulse to combine.
Taste and adjust seasoning — nasturtium adds its own peppery heat.
Serve immediately on pasta, grilled bread, or as a vegetable dip.
benefits: High in vitamin C from nasturtium
No store-bought ingredients needed when garden is in season
caution: Nasturtium is safe and edible for most people. Those on blood-thinning medications should avoid large quantities. Pregnant women should use in culinary (not medicinal) amounts only.
storageNotes: Cover with a thin layer of olive oil to prevent browning. Refrigerate up to 5 days. Freeze in ice cube trays for up to 6 months.

name: Coconut Lemongrass Garden Soup
description: A fragrant Thai-inspired soup using lemongrass, ginger, and vegetables fresh from your Zone 10b garden. This soup showcases how many of your garden plants can come together in one beautiful, healing dish.
ingredients: 3 stalks lemongrass, bruised and cut into 2-inch pieces
1 cup mixed vegetables (peppers, sweet potato, zucchini — from garden)
2 tbsp fish sauce or soy sauce
2 hot peppers (bird's eye or your garden variety), sliced
Fresh holy basil leaves to garnish
instructions: Bruise lemongrass by pressing with back of a knife to release oils.
Bring coconut milk and broth to a gentle simmer in a large pot.
Add lemongrass, ginger, and hot peppers. Simmer 10 minutes.
Add your garden vegetables and mushrooms. Cook until just tender, 8-10 minutes.
Season with fish sauce/soy sauce, lime juice, and sugar.
Taste and adjust — should be a balance of salty, sour, slightly sweet, and spicy.
Remove lemongrass pieces before serving.
Ladle into bowls. Top with fresh holy basil, scallions, and a squeeze of lime.
benefits: Full of vitamins from garden vegetables
Warm and comforting for cool season
caution: Adjust hot pepper amount to your heat preference. Check for coconut milk allergy. Fish sauce contains sodium — reduce if monitoring salt intake.
storageNotes: Keeps refrigerated for 3 days. Reheat gently — do not boil after adding coconut milk or it may separate.
